content: {"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12471658/", "source_title": "Assembly and Interconnection Technologies for 3D Plastic Circuit Carriers", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.62, "overall_relevance": 0.18, "year": 2025, "authors": [], "venue": "PMC", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "In-mold electronics manufacturing can lead to 70% reduction in weight and up to 90% reduction in thickness compared to conventional PCB techniques, with component bonding relying on conductive adhesives cured below 150 degrees Celsius rather than solder paste, and thermoforming executed at 100-160 degrees Celsius.", "direct_quote": "This method can lead to a 70% reduction in weight and up to 90% reduction in thickness compared to conventional techniques. Component bonding in IME relies on conductive adhesives instead of solder paste. The biggest advantage is that the curing temperature can normally remain lower than 150 °C. The vacuum forming is executed around 100–160 °C for most use cases, but this can vary a lot depending on the type of substrates", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.2, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Emerging_Trends", "entities": ["in-mold electronics", "70% weight reduction", "90% thickness reduction", "conductive adhesives", "150°C", "thermoforming", "100-160°C"]}, {"statement": "The most commonly used polymer substrates for 3D electronics are polycarbonate (85%) and PET (10%), with typical molding temperatures ranging from 190-220 degrees Celsius for polypropylene to 260-340 degrees Celsius for polycarbonate, and thermal expansion mismatch during cooling can lead to stresses in components and connections.", "direct_quote": "The most used polymer types are PC (85%) and PET (10%), but other types (5%) like ABS, PC/ABS, PMMA, TPU, PS, PE, PBT, PA6, and PA66 are also used. As the molded IME structure cools, a thermal expansion mismatch can lead to stresses in the components and their connections. Plastic materials are highly bendable and potentially stretchable, transparent, and emissive, which makes them ideal for flexible electronics", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.15, "confidence": 0.82, "perspective": "Industry", "entities": ["polycarbonate", "85%", "PET", "10%", "thermal expansion mismatch", "flexible electronics", "molding temperature"]}], "evidence_summary": "Review of 3D plastic circuit carrier technologies.
